metadata.title: What AI Hiring Systems Actually Measure (It's Not Merit)

beats.narration_text: Sawubona — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'The Algorithm as Gatekeeper: What AI Hiring Systems Actually Measure' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. What does an AI hiring system actually measure? The short answer: it measures what predicted success in the past. And the past contained inequities about who got hired and promoted. The AI does not introduce bias — it calcifies bias. Sixty percent of large companies now use AI systems that score facial expressions and speech patterns, often without a human in the room.

props.segment: What AI Hiring Systems Actually Measure (It's Not Merit)
props.command: What does an AI hiring system actually measure?
output: Trained on 10 years of successful hires — inherits who was hired and promoted.
'Bias drift': AI calcifies historical inequities, does not introduce new ones.
60% of large companies score facial expressions and speech without a human in the room.

beats.narration_text: Here is the feedback loop. Past hires — shaped by the historical biases of who got interviews, who got offers, who got promoted — become training data. The model learns what a successful hire looks like. That model makes future hiring decisions. Future hires reinforce the model's definition of success. The model is retrained on the new data. The cycle repeats. Each loop compresses the signal slightly further toward the historical pattern. The term for this is bias drift — not that the model introduces new bias, but that it amplifies and re-amplifies the existing bias until the historical inequity becomes a present-day system property.

manim.description: Flywheel diagram. Nodes in a circle: 'historical hires' → 'training data' → 'AI hiring model' → 'future hires' → back to 'training data'. Arrow labeled 'bias amplified each loop'. Disparity ratio counter in corner, climbing with each revolution.

beats.narration_text: The model never saw a demographic field. No race column. No gender column. It learned which zip codes correlate with candidates who received offers and promotions. Zip code is a legitimate feature — it correlates with commute time, local university quality, years of industry exposure. It also correlates, strongly, with race, because residential segregation shaped which zip codes are which. The model learned the correlation without learning the mechanism. It has no idea it is using race as a signal. The zip code threshold becomes a demographic filter. The bias was laundered through a legitimate feature.

animated_deck.description: Scatter plot: zip codes on x-axis, historical hire rate on y-axis. Threshold line drawn at a point. Left of threshold: low-hire zip codes, colored differently. Annotation: 'correlates with race via residential segregation'. Feature appears legitimate; demographic effect appears below.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The model never saw a demographic field. No race column. No gender column. It learned which zip codes correlate with candidates who received offers and promotio

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Sixty percent of large companies use AI hiring systems scoring facial expressions and speech without a human in the room. Bias drift means the AI calcifies historical inequities rather than introducing new ones — which makes it harder to detect, because the model can point to the correlation and call it legitimate. The model learned which zip codes correlate with success without a demographic field in the training data. The algorithm has become the authority on what merit looks like — and merit, it turns out, looks like whoever got hired before.

props.artifactTitle: What AI Hiring Systems Actually Measure (It's Not Merit)
artifactLines: 60% of large companies: AI scores facial expressions and speech, no human in room.
'Bias drift': AI calcifies historical inequities — harder to detect, not new.
Zip code as proxy: correlates with race via residential segregation — no demographic field needed.
The algorithm became the authority on merit. Merit looks like whoever was hired before.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Audit an AI hiring system's training data. What demographic proxies might appear in legitimate features like zip code, university name, or job title pattern?' University name is the same structure as zip code — a legitimate feature that correlates with demographic characteristics via historical access patterns. Job title patterns: the same. Run this audit on any structured hiring dataset you can access. Document which features are legitimate on their face and demographic proxies in effect. Liam, in for Bear.

props.segment: What AI Hiring Systems Actually Measure (It's Not Merit)
props.command: Audit an AI hiring system's training data. What demographic proxies might appear in 'legitimate' features like zip code, university name, or job title pattern?
